title: vimeo_1179379299
displayTitle: 2026-04-02 - Environment Committee - Environment (Disestablishment of Ministry for the Environment) Amendment Bill
topic: Environment (Disestablishment of Ministry for the Environment) Amendment Bill
description: Thursday 2 April 2026 | Environment Committee Environment (Disestablishment of Ministry for the Environment) Amendment Bill Hearing of evidence: List of submitters: Animal Justice Party Aotearoa New Zealand Danette Wereta Jenny Easton Bruce Gilkison Environment and Conservation Organisations of Aotearoa-NZ Barry Weeber, Chairperson Pirirakau Tribal Authority - Incorporated Heta Gardiner, Kaihautū - General Manager WWF New Zealand Jamie Fowler, Conservation Impact Manager Lillian Fougere, Principal Policy Advisor, Conservation Impact Troy Baisden Royal Forest and Bird Society of New Zealand Richard Capie, Chief Advisor Erika Toleman, Acting Chief Executive Zoran Rakovic Southern Young Labour Liam Calder, Vice-Chair Nelson Tasman Climate Forum David Bartle Fred Overmars Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment Simon Upton, Commissioner Geoff Simmons, Chief Advisor - Strategy and Policy Rebecca Thornley, General Counsel
descriptionSummary: Environment (Disestablishment of Ministry for the Environment) Amendment Bill. List of. Animal Justice Party Aotearoa New Zealand

chunks.text: Zealand, and we can see you there, Danette. Kia ora. Welcome to the select committee. Look, you have five minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission. Thank you. Thank you. So tēnā koutou katoa. Thank you for the opportunity to speak. My name, as you heard, is Danette Whetā.

I'm representing the Animal Justice Party, Aotearoa, New Zealand. We are a political party committed to advocating for the rights, welfare, and protection of animals. So we oppose the Environment's Disestablishment Amendment Bill. But firstly, I will acknowledge the government's intent to make improvements across housing, infrastructure, and the environment.

They genuinely do have complex challenges, and integrated thinking does have real value. So this proposal is just not an administration restructure, it is a decision about how environmental protection is prioritized within government, and whether it remains a clear, independent voice.

So removing that independence and placing those responsibilities within the broader ministry does create tension. Addressing housing and infrastructure challenges cannot come at the expense of environmental protection. So you're asking the same ministry to build the motorway and protect the wetland that the motorway destroys. That's not coordination, that's a conflict of interest written into an org chart.

These portfolios are not neutral. They are among the primary drivers of habitat destruction, freshwater and ecosystem decline. So the government has suggested that this change will improve coordination. However, coordination already occurs and can be strengthened with process. So disestablishing a dedicated ministry is not required to achieve that. As an experienced senior leader, I can advise structural change does cost time and a lot of money and doesn't fix problems.

I recommend identifying the root cause of the issues and providing solutions to address them. Now, importantly, environmental protection and animal welfare are inseparable, so animals rely on stable ecosystems for survival. That's their food, shelter, breeding, and safety, and when these systems are weakened, it's animals who pay the price. We've already seen this. Aotearoa is facing ongoing biodiversity loss and environmental pressure, and globally, we know that ecosystem decline is one of the leading drivers of species

decline. And there are broader implications. So New Zealand's reputation is tightly close to how we care for our environment, and that reputation underpins trust in our clean, green billion-dollar image. Equally important is accountability, so a standalone ministry provides a clear line of responsibility when things go wrong.

So 70% of freshwater fish are threatened with extinction or at risk, 78% of native land birds and 93% of native frogs and 94% of native reptiles are threatened or at risk. Nearly half of our monitored river sites fail to meet E. coli safe drinking water standards, and half of our lakes are in poor or degraded health due to nutrient contamination.

The ministry has been responsible for bringing this kind of clear, evidence-based reporting to light, and when that voice is absorbed, there is a real risk of this level of transparency and visibility being diminished over time. So we urge you to protect this proposal and instead of broad structural change, the focus should be on targeted, practical improvements that strengthen environmental outcomes.

We have a responsibility to do better. Thank you. Thank you very much, Danette, for your submission. Appreciate your time. It appears we have no questions, but we have got your written submission as well. Well, it's a very clear submission. Yeah, very clear, very passionate.
So we appreciate your time this morning. Thank you. Yeah. Kia ora. So we will now move to Jenny Easton.

Who I believe is joining us by-- Oh, there we go. Jenny, we can see you there. Welcome to the select committee this morning. Look, you've got five minutes with us, so in the essence of time, we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission. Thank you.

I began my written submission by explaining how important MFE's technical knowledge, independent voice, and international standing were for me, a resource scientist with Tasman District Council, which is a small unitary authority covering mainly rural land and a raft of complex contaminated historic sites.

MFE scientists established the Contaminated Sites Remediation Fund for the cleanup of the Māpua fruit growers' chemical site. They also provided nationwide technical advice across, and access to high temperature incineration in France through the Basel Convention for the disposal of hazardous waste such as DDT, dieldrin, PCBs, from the redundant agrochemical collection. And without this technical support from MFE, farmers and industries would not have supported this drive by councils to remove toxic waste for liability

issues. The parameters for environmental reporting are complex. Ozone layer protection, waste minimization all require specialist technical knowledge, which was provided by MFE staff. Now I question, would this still be available if MFE staff were buried inside a mega ministry?

And I seriously doubt that for the following reasons. A well-funded standalone ministry has a louder, stronger, more authoritative voice, and it can take environmental concerns to the Cabinet table.And this makes it possible to recruit and retain high-quality staff and preserve the status of the ministry, build institutional knowledge, and with the status and professional relationships, have access to overseas experts for the emerging issues, those unknowns which are arriving here soon. And this status and mana will be lost if the

Secretary for the Environment functions are given to the CEO of the mega ministry. And then there's the climate work. The staff at MFE already have a battle to provide advice and policies to meet our climate responsibilities for emission reduction when the Climate Commission's advice is not accepted and the ETS has been altered in such a way it's no longer effective. We need to move to a low carbon economy and protect the carbon sinks, and we need the ministry staff to guide us.

Work on climate adaptation plan is very complex and politically challenging, and with the actual process of merging the ministry, that will take the staff's focus away from these projects, and without a clear national directive, local and central government staff are managing the recovery from climatic weather events in a very ad hoc manner.

And as you know, this is unsustainable, it's very expensive, and it's inherently unfair. MFE is far too important for our future to merge and weaken its voice for nature and our physical and natural environment. We need its independent oversight to support, protect, and improve our economy and wellbeing.

And as a last note, I hope that the NEB and planning bills are changed to include a goal for emission reduction targets, goals, and plans under the Climate Change Response Act 2002. If this doesn't happen, the role of MFE will be even more challenging and difficult if they're part of a mega ministry where their voice will be diluted as they compete with transport and urban development. Yes, I've finished.

Thank you very much, Jenny, for your submission today. We appreciate your time and the effort that you've made to provide that submission. So thank you. Thank you very much. Thank you. It's very helpful. Very helpful. Good point about the screening. Thank you. Thank you, Jenny.
So now we'll move on to our next submission today, Bruce Gilkison.
Why do I keep back?

So we just got Bruce popping up. There we go. Bruce, welcome to the select committee this morning. You have five minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you to open up with some opening remarks and talk you through your submission, talk us through your submission. Oh, we just got something to share with us there. Yes, brilliant.

Thank you. Over to you. Okay. Kia ora, and thank you very much. I am with Paul Martin. So my background is in business and accounting. His is in health and disability, and he will speak if I get tongue-tied. We will say we disagree with the disestablishment proposal, but if that is to proceed, we will have some suggestions in relation to how that option might be improved.

So that's the second slide we're looking at there. I also do some research and writing on environmental issues, mainly for chartered accountants in New Zealand and Australia for their monthly journal. The image is an early involvement from 26 years ago. I've included it here for the strong cross-party support it attracted.

It was launched by Sir Geoffrey Palmer and included a foreword from Simon Upton, who was then Minister for the Environment. I'm pleased to say that climate and nature are now a central focus for Chartered Accountants Australia and New Zealand. Next slide, please. Talking about urgency, this is the Planetary Boundaries framework, which many of you will be familiar with, largely developed by scientists at the Stockholm Resilience Centre.

Seven of the nine safe global limits have now been transgressed, so it's urgent. Climate is one of the boundaries. Despite some progress recently, the rate of emissions globally is still speeding up. A few comments. As we now know, the past 11 years are the hottest on record.

Everything is connected. Some impacts cause feedback loops, resulting in further damage elsewhere. So heat can lead to forest fires and dieback, which releases carbon, which leads to more heat. Limits once breached can be irreversible, and meanwhile, every fraction of a degree really matters.

Next, the globally trusted UK-based Institute and Faculty of Actuaries, using methods tried and trusted to manage and advise on trillions of dollars in pensions, investments, and insurance, has concluded that risks to planetary solvency have been hugely underestimatedAnd that without rapid action on climate and environmental risks, the global economy could face a 50% loss in GDP, and yes, that's 50%, between 2070 and

2090. So they're saying it's even worse than we thought. Sorry. And that better information, clear limits, and better policies are urgently needed everywhere. The report is a call to action and is highly recommended. Next, please. Here I'm saying that extra costs to retain MFE's independence, and I don't know what they are or indeed what the benefits are, will be hugely offset by environmental and other benefits.

So first, there is ample evidence of potential gains, and I've focused here mainly on dollars, not just on environmental benefits. Here are two sources for now. Modeling by Deloitte New Zealand showed that decisive climate action could add 64 billion to our economy by 2050.

And a report from the Sustainable Business Council and Climate Leaders Coalition this week shows that climate-friendly technology could see GDP grow by 22 billion per year by 2035. It could also help prevent declines in agriculture and tourism revenue.

Some other benefits I've noted here are savings from mitigating and preparing wisely for future climate damage, avoiding possible reputational damage and risks to free trade agreements from inadequate emissions progress, and avoiding some future significant health costs.

For each of those, it's better and cheaper to protect something rather than try to fix it later. MFE's independence, expertise, clarity of purpose, and ability to act and advise transparently are valuable and needed like never before. Slide, please. I'm wondering if MFE is to merge, is the new ministry the right place for it?

And I'm just looking at impacts and benefits from two major blocks. So, question is where could that expertise provide most benefit? And on this basis, though I'm not recommending this, there could potentially be greater benefits in aligning with the latter.

I consider that the merger should certainly proceed, but without MFE, and at least not with more detailed research into the benefits and risks for current and future New Zealanders. Slide, please. This one's a bit flippant, but I'm just talking about the benefits of having an independent voice. So, yeah, rather than have an environment agency in one section, but to better to act transparently, to be able to advise, encourage, and guide all sectors, and particularly those

which are large emitters, have significant impacts, or are presenting major opportunities for progress and better outcomes. Next, please. I mentioned the heading, which probably should have been headed Items to be Addressed if the Bill Proceeds, and if we do this, how do we make sure that nothing gets lost, rather than the heading Amendments, because some of these are outside the bill itself. For instance, some items are covered by the RMA reforms currently underway.

The focus on the status of the Secretary for the Environment's role and the priority of MFE's increasingly urgent climate and environmental responsibilities are key concerns. It's crucial that nothing diminishes or detracts from these, and there be no loss of momentum through what could be a turbulent period if the full merger were to occur as proposed. As noted, I consider that the retention of MFE as an independent ministry is likely to bring about far better outcomes for people and planet.

And final slide, please. That one looks as if it got lost a bit at the top there. But with multiple and escalating impacts now inevitable, this is the wrong time to risk weakening a clear and focused and independent voice for the environment.

I consider that even with some refinement on the proposal, unless compelling evidence is available to the contrary, a change at this stage could result in disruption, dilution, and diminished transparency. Meanwhile, retaining and bolstering this voice, in my view, is the best way to mitigate and prepare for negative impacts and to plan for and seize significant future opportunities.

New Zealanders, current and future, love and depend on their natural environment for their livelihoods and their well-being. Please support them. Thank you. We welcome any questions. Thank you, Bruce, and thank you for those slides. That's really useful and helpful. Look, we've got a couple of questions.

We'll go to Priyanka first. Thank you. Thanks, Bruce. I've just got a quick question seeking clarification around the first amendment that you have suggested to explicitly prioritize environmental protection as a primary function if this merger were to go ahead. Other submitters have made the point that they feel that even though the bill as it stands says that all the functions would transfer over to the new ministry, that the focus on the natural environment might fall away as a result. So presumably, you are talking about prioritizing both the

natural and built environments in that amendment that you've suggested. Yes. Yeah. Wanting to make sure that it's protected and bolstered. There are a lot of threats coming. The next few years are going to be perhaps quite tumultuous, and certainly not wanting anything to be lost there.

Thank you. Thank you. And Rachel Brooking. Oh, yes. Just a quick question. Thank you for the history and for your involvement all that time ago. I'm interested in the international experience you have, and you've just said, "I believe it is just a matter of time before we are challenged by countries and producers which are now far more environmentally focused than we are."

What countries do you think are far more environmentally focused now than New Zealand is? Yes, I'm probably referring there to UK and EU, which certainly they have a strong focus, for instance, in our free trade agreements on not backing off on climate progress.

And I think it could easily happen that perhaps farmers in those areas might raise concerns. And yeah, I think any further changes there could put us at risk. Thank you. Brilliant. Well, thank you very much, Bruce. We've met time there with our submissions this morning, but we appreciate your time and the really insightful, useful submission.

So thank you. Thank you. Like the pictures, the pictures of the All Blacks and the ... I don't know if we won that one. Yeah. Thank you. All right. Thanks. I thought it was going to be that game with France. Yeah. Oh, yes. Yeah. Thanks very much.
So moving on to our next submission this morning, Environment and Conservation Organizations of Aotearoa, New Zealand.

Who are also joining us online and hopefully popping through shortly. There we go. Oh, we can see you there, Barry. Welcome to the select committee this afternoon. Look, you have 10 minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission, and then we'll open up for a few questions.

Thank you. Thank you. Kia ora koutou. Thanks to the committee to let us present our submission. I assume you've had a read our submission already. I'll introduce myself. I'm Barry Weber. I'm the chairperson of the Environment Conservation Organizations. As you've seen in our submission, ECO's had a long history and involvement in this area. We were involved in advocacy for the establishment of the Ministry of the Environment back in the 1980s.

For those who don't know, that came out of the Environment Forum, which was established in 1985, where a number of issues clearly identified with the lack of environmental priority of the governments at that stage and the failures that the Think Big projects had occurred, and the costs and losses that were associated with those projects on the environment.

In addition, the lack of focus on environmental issues generally and the idea that we need to bring in together all the different green dots in the different agencies at the time, and the Commission for the Environment, as it then was, was only a agency with a cabinet minute. It didn't have any statutory functions or statutory basis for its decision-making.

So the Environment Act was a really important part of that, and I should note that this is the 40th year of the Environment Act. It was passed in December of 1986, so the Ministry for the Environment, if it still exists at the end of this year, would have reached its 40th year. So that's, I think, it's an important milestone in environment policy in New Zealand that we should not forget.

So we're concerned about the lack of environmental focus. You've heard from other submitters this morning about that, and we're concerned about the lack of environmental focus of the government generally and the need for recognition that the environment is a key important part of New Zealand in terms of our loss of biodiversity, our state of the environment, which is declining, and we need to have a lot greater focus on it. And we're concerned that this piece of legislation would make and weaken that environmental focus.

We also note that yesterday the government released its priorities for science funding, which also shows a huge reduction in environmental science funding. And again, that doesn't augur well for the future of New Zealand's environment or the government and other agencies making clear decisions on the environment, because science funding is a key part of that equation.

We're also concerned that we're currently talking about this bill, but a new position has been appointed. The head of corrections has been appointed to this position of running an agency which Parliament is yet to pass legislationTo enforce. So it's a bit of an odd way of doing things, and we hope that this is an open and transparent process that Parliament is going through, and the executive isn't overriding its parliamentary mandate in that process.

We're also concerned that the Cabinet papers associated with this document and this decision are heavily redacted, and I don't know whether Parliament itself has actually looked at the full detail of those Cabinet papers or asked for copies of the unredacted copies of those papers that were released. And we would urge the select committee to ask for those papers so that they can review all the documentation and the reasons for the decisions being made.

The other problem is that the legislation itself misses part of the role of the Ministry for the Environment and different agencies. We haven't mentioned to it, but I would suggest that the drafters look at the Fisheries Act, for example, as one piece of legislation which mentions the Ministry for the Environment, which isn't included.

And so there are potentially other examples. We haven't looked through the full details, but clearly the draft of this bill was done in a hurry without clear thought and consideration of all the references to the environment agency in different pieces of legislation. We're concerned about the transparency of advice that will be with this new agency. We've seen in other agencies where there's been accumulation of different ministries, for example, Ministry for Primary Industry. We've noticed the lack of transparency in terms of

some fisheries advice and the way that occurs within an agency and the sort of priorities for funding within that agency. So it's really important that whatever comes out of this process, that there's clear transparency between the different parts, if it is combined, different parts of the agency, so we can see where officials disagree or officials agree and what priorities different parts of the agency have, especially when they're acting under different statutes.

That's a clear summary of our submission. We don't support this bill. We urge the select committee not to recommend this bill be passed, and that in fact, that we move forward and reconsider the role of the Ministry for the Environment in the future.

Thank you, Chair, and I'm happy to answer any questions that the committee may have in regard to our submission. Thanks. Thank you, Barry. Look, we'll go first to Rachel Brooking. Yeah, thanks, Barry, and thanks for reminding us of National's blueprint, Cleaning Up Freshwater and the like. I often go back to that document.

It does seem rather inconsistent with everything that we've seen before this committee this term. But my question for you is more specifically on seven, where you talk about when the Environment Act was passed, that there was supposed to be environmental impact assessment provisions, and where you see that best sitting into all of the environment scheme, also noting that we've got the Planning Bill and the Natural Environment Bill in front of us too.

Well, I don't know whether the select committee have heard of the Environmental Protection and Enhancement Procedures. Probably not. They were established in the late '70s and revised during the '80s. They were wider than environmental assessment for projects. They had provisions for assessment of policies and actions of agencies rather than just actual consent conditions.

So while the Resource Management Act might be argued to replace some of those provisions of the Environmental Protection and Enhancement Procedures, not all the provisions of those procedures were replaced. And I'd urge the select committee to get yourself a copy of the EP and EP procedures. I'm assuming that they'll be in Parliamentary Library. They're not something that you can find online.

I actually looked online to see whether I could find them, but you can't these days. They were actually a very forward-looking piece of document. They were originally produced under the national government in the Muldoon years and were revised later on through the 1980s. And the original intention of the Environment Act was that basically part three of the Environment Act would contain the Environmental Protection and Enhancement Procedures, but that never occurred. Despite early decisions by Cabinet to do that, that was never proceeded with.

And I think that's a great omission, because as I said, the Resource Management Act and other legislation only fulfill part of the provisions that the EP and EP procedures actually covered. Thanks. Thank you. Thank you very much, Barry. We appreciate your submission today and your insights.

Lots of considerations to take into account there. So thank you very much. Thank you. Thank you, Chair. Thank you, committee. Look forward to your decisions. Thanks. So now we move on to our next submitter, the Pirirakau Tribal Authority, who are here with us in person. Welcome.

Welcome to the select committee this afternoon now. Look, you've got 10 minutes with us, but take your submission as read, and if you'd like to just talk us through your main points, and then we'll open up for a few questions. So over to you. Ka pai. Kia ora tatou. Tuatahi ake ka mihi ki a koutou, e te komiti whiriwhiri, te komiti whaiti, i whakawātea mai te wāhi ki a mātou o te Pirirakau ki te kōrero ki te kaupapa nei. Tēnā koutou.

Kia ora tātou. My name is Heta Gardiner. I'm speaking on behalf of Pirirākau Tribal Authority as our Kaihautū. For generations, Pirirākau Hapū have been the kaitiaki of our rohe in the western Bay of Plenty, and our connection with the whenua, with our waters is more than just cultural.

This is fundamental to our very identity and survival. So I'm here to speak in firm opposition to the environment, this establishment of the Ministry of the Environment Amendment Bill. We view this bill not just as a bureaucratic reshuffle, as has been suggested, but as a direct, calculated attack on Māori rights and the protection of Aotearoa's greatest asset, our natural environment.

Tuatahi ake, the bill is in breach of Article 2 of Te Tiriti o Waitangi, which guarantees us tino rangatiratanga over our taonga. More specifically to us of Te Pirirākau, the bill threatens to hollow out the Ngā Hapu o Ngāti Ranginui Claims Settlement Act 2025. That settlement was "a living promise from the government on behalf of the Crown to acknowledge the past wrongs and protect our taonga for future generations." The Crown apologized for the unjust confiscation of our lands and promised a new relationship based on

mutual trust. Yet here we are, less than a year later, watching the government unilaterally, without any external consultation with Treaty partners, getting ready to dismantle the very agency responsible for upholding those relationships and protecting the aforementioned taonga. A commitment made must be a commitment kept. You can't deliver on Treaty settlements if you scatter the expertise and relationships required to honor them.

We question the logic of merging ministries that appear to have minimal intersecting areas of mutual alignment. We also question the sense and value of having a mega ministry in the first place. These are entities, keeping in mind, that are tasked with overseeing the most important areas in our society.

Throwing them together to make a mega ministry seems like a knee-jerk reaction to save money in the short term. When speaking of the justification of the merge of several ministries, I've seen terms like improved coordination be used, terms like align policymaking across sectors, terms like reduce duplication and bureaucracy.

This is political speak for downsizing. Downsizing several different areas into one, in this case ministries, results always in a dilution of expertise and ultimately a reduction in financial support. We can't do that for the environment. It's too important.

Having priorities such as economic development and environmental protections competing with each other within the same ministry weakens the advancement of both of them. And while we speak of economic development and financial responsibility, defunding and deprioritizing the environment doesn't save us money in the long term. It just pushes the bill that we all ultimately have to pay down the road.

For example, Cyclone Gabrielle needed over $10 billion of financial support for resilience and recovery. The earthquakes in Kaikōura, billions of dollars needed for recovery and resilience. The 2023 floods in Auckland, billions of dollars needed for recovery and resilience. And those are just some examples.

We live within an ecosystem here in Aotearoa that is heavily reliant on meat exports, dairy exports, fruit exports that need a functioning, not collapsing environment. Tourism, agriculture, fisheries depend entirely on clean water and a healthy environment.

For us at Pirirākau, horticulture is a major source of income, and we worry about what the space will look like in the absence of proactive environmental leadership from our government and from our ministries. Saving millions now for it to only cost us billions later, to me, is not sound economic strategy. It's fiscally irresponsible.

And there's much more at stake here, of course, than just money. We in Te Pirirākau, we share our space with the moana, and there's grave concern for our urupā and our people in the case of a major weather event. "Ko au te taiao, ko te taiao ko au" I am the environment, the environment is me.

This proverb doesn't only encapsulate the relationship Māori have with the environment, but also a pathway forward where we can both thrive. And so I'll conclude my korero with the question of the government finds itself in this situation, pushing against long-term sustainability, pushing against environmental protections, and pushing against long-term economic sound decision-making, then I would question the wisdom of that position.

And I will just conclude by saying, Te Pirirākau Tribal Authority stand in opposition of this bill. Kia ora tātou. Thank you very much. Look, we've got a question from Cameron Luxton. Yeah. Thank you, Heta. Got a question. You talked about, I think the quote was, "It's hollowing out the Ngāti Ranginui Crown Settlement Bill," and the relationship with the Crown.

Is the settlement with the Crown or the ministry? Because I- The Crown... looking through this... Yeah. So looking through the settlement bill, well- There's no mention of the Ministry for the Environment But the Ministry of Environment is the entity that oversees and ultimately looks after the protections and that relationship.

A diminishing of the ministry, in our view, ultimately results in a diminishing of the environment, which is where that conflict occurs. So would the Crown ever be able to change the makeup of ministries and not breach the Ngati Ranginui settlement bill?

Of course, but this is ultimately, again, as we said, ultimately this bill will result in, as I said, a dilution of expertise and a reduction of funds for the environment, and that has an impact. Yeah. So supplementary on this point. So I think your point is, tell me, the question is, am I right or wrong with what I'm saying? Is that all these issues on your back page here of your bill go to the diminishment of environmental protections, and it is because of that, as a consequence of MFE being abolished, that go to your

settlement issue. Is that right? The ultimate point is that a diminishing of the ministry diminishes the capabilities to look after the environment. And by diminishing the capabilities to look after the environment and our ability to look after our taonga, that ultimately is the conflict.

Yeah. Which is much more eloquently put than my question. Thank you. Look, thank you very much for your time today. I appreciate your submission and your passion as well. So thank you. And so we now call our next submitter, WWF New Zealand, who are also here and joining us in person today.

Welcome to the select committee this afternoon. You have five minutes with us, and so we'll just hand it over to you to open up and talk us through your submission, and then we'll have hopefully a few questions. Great. Thank you for the opportunity to speak to you all today. I'm Jamie Fowler. I'm the conservation impact manager at WWF New Zealand, and I'm here with Lillian Fougère, who's our principal policy advisor.

We understand that this bill doesn't change the core functions of the Ministry for the Environment, but we are concerned about how those functions will operate inside a much broader new ministry. Bringing environment together with strong development, housing, transport, and infrastructure mandates risks creating built-in conflicts that could weaken the ministry's ability to carry out its core environmental protection role.

New Zealand's biodiversity is already in crisis, with some of the highest rates of extinction in the world, driven by habitat loss, pollution, and cumulative impacts. The Ministry for the Environment currently provides essential safeguards for nature. Those safeguards cannot be diluted by competing goals like road construction or urban expansion.

As we know, nature underpins our health, our wellbeing, our climate resilience, and our economy. Primary industries and tourism rely heavily on healthy ecosystems, and if the environmental mandate is weakened, those foundations erode and future generations will bear that cost. The Environment Act requires the ministry to consider things like ecosystem health, threatened species, landscapes, areas of importance to tangata whenua, pollution, natural hazards, hazardous substances, and cumulative effects.

These are not optional extras. They are core stewardship duties. We are concerned that within a much larger ministry, these functions would be overshadowed by the development priorities. The bill doesn't provide clarity or safeguards to show how these tensions will be managed. Without explicit direction, environmental protection risks becoming secondary. We also challenge the assumption that this merger will create efficiency. Combining such different mandates blurs organizational purpose, confuses advice to ministers, shifts

conflicts to inside one agency rather than resolving them between clear, specialized ministries. This is likely to slow decisions, advice, and internal negotiations. Far from creating efficiency, it risks making the system less effective than what we have now. WWF New Zealand does not support the bill or the merger as proposed.

However, if the merger proceeds, we strongly recommend three things. First, strengthen the bill so environmental functions cannot be weakened through secondary legislation or organizational restructuring. Environmental protection, biodiversity, and ecosystem health must be explicitly prioritized for the new priorities for the new ministry's purpose and powers.

Second, ensure that the Secretary for the Environment keeps an independent, authoritative role, able to give free and frank environmental advice, supported by staff who have genuine environmental expertise and experience. This is particularly important in the context of the RMA replacement work, which will take very many years to enact and implement.

Third, improve transparency and accountability. The annual reporting, statutory environmental limits, and independent oversight must be strong enough to prevent environmental outcomes being compromised. To conclude, Aotearoa's environment is globally unique and under immense pressure. It needs strong, clear institutional guardianship, and we urge the committee to ensure the bill and any related instruments explicitly protect the integrity of the environmental mandate within the new ministry.

Thank you for your time, and we're happy to answer any inquiries. Thank you very much for that submission. Priyanka has the first question. Thank you. You've mentioned in your written submission your concerns around the conflicts between development and environmental protection, which seems to me to be quite similar to the trade-offs that PCE has mentioned in his submission.

Would you go so far as he has to say that you would suggest that MFE is left out of the merger? I would. I think the transparency and visibility of those trade-offs is incredibly important. Mm-hmm. The reason why MFE was established in the first place was to drive the quality decision making and informed decision making and evidence to fully understand those impacts of our decisions on the environment, and that still remains an important function.

And Rachel, just- Yes. To follow up. Yeah. So when you say there needs to be visibility, part of what he has said is that the risk is that the trade-offs would be managed within this mega ministry and not elevated- That's right... to ministers, and that's what you mean by that statement? That's right, yes. The contestable advice needs to be, yeah.
Thank you. Explicit. Thank you very much for your submission today. We appreciate those considerations, so thank you. Thanks. Thank you.

So we now move to our next submitter, Troy Baisden. Troy, welcome to the Select Committee this afternoon. Yeah. You have five minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission. Thank you. Tena koutou, tena koutou katoa.

Thanks for hearing this. I am an environmental scientist, working across science and policy. I've been on MFE's Interim Limits and Targets Advisory Group, as well as the MB group that advised government on the science system under the last group.

The areas I want to focus on here are the transparency of science and research that tells us how the environment actually is and how it's doing with the statutory functions of the Secretary of the Environment and parts that were set up under the original environment legislation. That legislation was sort of world leading at the time, and is now the norm to have a Ministry for the Environment or a similar environmental agency at a top level of government.

That shouldn't be a surprise to anyone, and bringing people like me to New Zealand 25 years ago from Ivy League universities in Berkeley or simply looking after our trade and international reputation is essential. Having the role of the Ministry for the Environment within that is essential.

I think everybody can see that to some degree. The question here is will this bill actually do what it says, and what can be done around making the process here as effective as possible? I oppose the bill. I oppose this establishment of the Ministry for the Environment.

I would've worn a tie if I believed that I could actually convince you to go back on that. However, I think the more important thing here is something that, amongst others, the PCE has identified. There are core things that happen within government and core things that are set up in legislation where the Ministry for the Environment is relatively unique.

My observation is that a lot of those go badly in super ministries, and that's certainly what I've observed with the super ministries like MB that have been created. I can see benefits from remixing the planning functions of MFE with other ministries. I can't see benefits from disestablishing the roles of MFE as seen in making them more invisible in a super ministry. There are two big problems to address, one of which that I would really emphasize is making as much as possible here reversible. So when it's not seen as working or a different

government comes in, it's relatively smooth to fix the parts that aren't working and let the parts of the merger that maybe are working continue. One of the most important pieces of those for you to understand is the role of the chief executive, and we've now seen a chief executive appointment. And that person, as has come out in the media just in the last day, does not give me confidence that this is going to proceed as planned or successfully.

There's little more to say about that, so what I'll move to is the problem with ministries. You've probably seen this before. I'll certainly leave it as an exhibit. This is the unscrambling government diagram from the New Zealand Initiative. And how does science or reality negotiate this? I would hand it to you that a lot of the problems we have can be fixed by restructuring. But the restructuring might look more like this, which is Norway. And in this model, you might see things like climate, environment, and conservation put together rather

than environment, as we call it now, put in with planning and building and transport. I think there's some very different functions there, and I support the PCE's notion that these need to have a competition at the table within Cabinet. The last thing that I worry about, and I will encourage you to think about, and by looking around the table, I think you can understand this without me explaining it further. It's mentioned or is consistent with the New Zealand Initiative's Unscrambling Government report, and it's one of the other concerns about
restructurings like this that generate super ministries. They generate accountability sinks, which make government function worse rather than better.

So with that, I think that's really what I wanted to cover and we're close to five minutes anyway. I mean question. Yeah. Thank you, Troy. Look, we do have a question from Francesco. Yes. Cool. Thank you. Thank you, Chair. Can you elaborate on what you mean by how this new ministry could create an accountability sink with a concrete hypothetical example, if you could?
Wow. Concrete hypothetical.

Yeah. So, the typical way these accountability things work is that a decision is made, and it's not clear who's responsible for the decision. So let's say that that has to do with the DPSIR loop of monitoring the quality of, for example, fresh water, and then making decisions about land use that relate to that.

If, in fact, we are losing track of who's supposed to be monitoring fresh water and considering what that actually means, as well as who's responsible for the return to policy, that's an accountability problem, and it's already a problem because that DPSIR loop does not connect back until we implement the changes the PCE recommended, which I think are still ongoing.

Thank you very much, Troy, for your submission today. Really appreciate that. So thank you. Yes. Thank you. And if you get the Clarkson diagrams, that Norway one's interesting. Mm-hmm. Yeah. And then with the other one, I think it's in the New Zealand initiative reports that we already have. We now welcome our next submitter, the Royal Forest and Bird Society of New Zealand. Welcome, Richard, Erica.

Hello. How are you? He's got a great memory. Look, we- We're back again. You have five minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission. So thank you. Thank you. Tēnā koutou katoa. Thank you for the opportunity to appear again before you today. My name is Erica Tolman.

I'm acting CEO of Forest and Bird. We are Aotearoa New Zealand's independent voice for nature, and we have more than a century of experience advocating for biodiversity, fresh water, and the natural environment. Look, our position is that we do not support this bill. We recognize the value of better coordination across portfolios.

But disestablishing the Ministry for the Environment and embedding its statutory functions within a large development-oriented super ministry creates risks that outweigh any potential administrative benefits. So we have a couple of key concerns. First off is dilution of environmental leadership.

So the bill transfers MFE's statutory functions to a secretary within a consolidated ministry, as you're all aware. In practice, we say that that will risk diluting time, focus, and institutional weight for environmental stewardship. Another issue is the conflicting mandates and weaker advice.

So embedding environmental statutory functions alongside mandates to promote development, transport efficiency, and urban growth creates structural conflicts, and we're particularly concerned with some of the drafting here. So, Clause 12 of the bill amends Section 32 of the Environment Act, and it requires that the secretary considers those environmental matters listed as far as practical.

Now, I recognize that that wording's already in the Environment Act. But, the problem here is that it's only going to apply to the secretary, not all the officials that sit under that person. And also, it is going to be weighed up in the ministry against all the other competing directions.

So in our view, that will mean environmental considerations are diluted. Reduced transparency and contestability, you've already heard about that, so I'll just skim over that, and again, the defunding risk you've heard already from the last two submitters about the potential for that. I'll hand over to Richard to carry on. Hi.

Kia ora, everybody. I'm Richard Cappy. I'm the Chief Advisor for Forest and Bird. It's nice to see you all again. I think one of the things that we would flag for the committee is that there is some significant timing and implementation risks here that need to be taken into account. This reform has been proposed, as you will know from the mahi that you've been undertaking, while the environmental system is already undergoing massive change. We've got generational changes happening to the resource management system, including two new bills and

national directions. Those things are still being designed and will take a number of years to actually embed and secure. Furthermore, we have significant mahi taking place where much of the rubber hits the road, the regulatory environments in our local and regional government, where we are looking at potentially some significant reforms in that space as well. The risks posed by increasing this instability should not be understated.

There's that old adage that form should follow function. We haven't actually determined what those functions truly are yet, let alone tested them. Yeah. Yet we're looking at reshaping and fundamentally shifting a lot of the expertise that exists in this space. The institutional disruption that could take place at the very time that you probably need that stability in order to make RM reforms and regional and local government reforms successful should not be understated. You will risk losing specialist staff, you'll risk losing capability, and you'll risk losing institutional

knowledge. If you want RM3 to be successful and endure, then you should think very carefully about whether doing this at this particular time is the right idea. I think it poses real risks to effective implementation of significant changes that this government has already signaled that it wishes to take forward.

So if the bill proceeds, what should be the minimum safeguards? I think we've already said there should be statutory clarity, and as Erica and others have said, it clearly established the role and authority of a dedicated secretary for the environment. There should be strong obligations, including explicit statutory duties on the consolidated ministry to give effect to environmental principles, not optional or as far as practicable.

There should be ring-fenced funding, transparent, dedicated funding lines for environmental functions, and there should be visible contestable adviceMechanisms to ensure that environmental advice remains independent in practice and clearly visible to ministers, parliament, and the public.

Finally, I think at a practical level, there are drafting improvements that would need to be made and would suggest the work done by EDS and around this makes a good starting point. In summary, I think we're pretty clear that the committee should recommend against the bill in its current form. I think that risk around being able to drive forward RM3 is really, really critical, and one that I haven't seen picked up or given due consideration. If you were to proceed, I think the PCE is correct, keep MFE out.

Certainly at this point in time, we would agree with the parliamentary commissioner on that. But we would have concerns about the two bills being split across the ministry, so keeping part in with MFE and the risk with MERT or MERT or the Curt. Because we've already flagged quite deep concerns about actually the problematic nature of having two pieces of legislation for RM3 in the first place.

The reality is, and I guess I'm talking to the members of the coalition in a sense, that if you're looking for efficiency and you're looking for affordability and you're looking for effectiveness, there are better ways to achieve that. You can strengthen environmental leadership rather than subsume it. There are interdepartmental boards and strengthened coordination mechanisms that could be put in place.

Thank you for your time. Happy to answer any questions. Thank you, Richard and Erica, for that very clear submission. Rachel has a question. Yeah. Just if you can spend a tiny little bit more time on the relationship to what you're calling RM3, and that is the planning bill being separate from the Natural Environment Bill and the integration of the resource management system and environment in the system and how it links with this.

Sure. So, we have serious concerns about the ability or actually inability to do integrated management with the way that the two bills have been separated. Biodiversity is one issue that is very tricky to follow through the two bills and ensure that actually protections are going to be, one, effective for the environment and, two, actually efficient for resource users.

And that is, we can't see a way to easily fix that. We weren't able to suggest any fixes in our submissions on those bills because of the, in our view, arbitrary split between land and environment. They are the same thing and, in our view, should be managed as one.

The PCE had suggested, I think if I understand that submission correctly, that those two bills could be managed by different administering agencies. We would not agree with that for the reasons that I've just set out. Okay, well, thank you very much. Lan, actually, one quick question. Go for it.

I don't know if it's a quick question, but- It's never a quick question. We'll try and be quick to answer. So, I'm not sure if you caught hearings earlier this morning, but we had Professor Bronwyn Hayward from University of Canterbury talking about she really struggled to find international examples where these kind of mergers have actually taken place and then had better outcomes for the environment.

Mm-hmm. Are you aware with your international colleagues, in agencies around the world where things like this might have had better environmental outcomes? It's not something that I'm aware of. So I've worked in a number of different jurisdictions, and I've looked at a number of different other jurisdictions and their approaches. It's not something that I've come across.

I don't think it's been flagged in any of our reports by officials either. Okay. No. Thank you. Thank you very much. Thank you very much. Thanks. So we will now move to our next submitter, Zoran Rakovic, who is joining us on live and Zoom? Zoom online.

Zoom online, yeah. Oh, there we go. We can see you, Zoran. Welcome to the select committee this afternoon. We'll hand it over to you. You have five minutes with us for your submission, so over to you. Thank you, Madam Chair, members of the committee, thank you for the chance to speak. My name is Zoran Rakovic. I'm a retired structural engineer from Selwyn, and my submission is less of an expert's view than it's more of a common sense than lay person's view. Selwyn is one of the fastest growing districts in the country, so

what you are deciding here will land directly in our communities. I'm not necessarily against merging ministries if it means housing, transport, and environment are finally working together. That could be a good thing. But here's the risk in simple terms. When you put everything into one big department, the pressure to get things built can start to outweigh the need to get things right.

And in a place like Selwyn, that matters. We are growing fast. Rolleston, Lincoln, Prebbleton, new houses, new subdivisions. That puts pressure on stormwater, flood protection, groundwater, and roads. If those things aren't properly planned upfront, the problems don't disappear.

They show up later. Flooding, water quality issues, bigger costs to fix the infrastructure that should've been done properly the first time. And who pays? Local ratepayers. That's the real issue, not growth itself, but unfunded or poorly planned growthThis bill says the functions stay the same, just moved to a different structure. But structure matters.

It affects priorities. It affects how decisions are made day to day. If environmental expertise gets diluted, even slightly, those trade-offs won't be as visible. So my ask is simple. If you're going to merge these agencies, put some guard rails in place. Make sure there are still strong environmental experts in the system. Make sure decisions are transparent so people can see how trade-offs are made. And make sure that if development is sped up, the cost of things like drainage, flood protection, and water systems is properly accounted for, not pushed onto

existing communities later. Because the reality is very simple. Water still flows downhill. Pipes still have limits. Flood plains still flood. Changing the structure in Wellington doesn't change that. If this reform helps things line up better, great. But if it leads to cutting corners, Selwyn will be the one paying for it.

Thank you. Thank you very much, Zoran, for that submission. We appear to have no further questions on that, but we appreciate you taking the time to submit to us today. So thank you very much. I appreciate your time. Thank you. Very clear submission. So thank you.

Look, we're now going to move to Southern Young Labour. There we are. We can see you there. Welcome to the select committee this afternoon. Thank you, Madam Chair. You have five minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission. Thank you. Kia ora koutou, and thank you for allowing me the opportunity to speak today on behalf of Southern Young Labour.

My name is Liam Calder, and I'm the vice president. And on behalf of Southern Young Labour, we strongly oppose the environment or the disestablishment of the Ministry of Environment Amendment Bill. As representatives of rangatahi across the lower South Island, we speak for a generation that will live with the long-term consequences from the decisions that are made within the select committee room.

Environmental governance is not abstract to us, but it is the future that we will inherit. This bill proposes to disestablish the Ministry of Environment at a time when coordinated long-term environmental leadership is more critical than ever. The Ministry of Environment plays a central role in providing independent advice, coordinating across governments, coordinating across elections even, and ensuring consistency in environmental policy. Without it, responsibility can remain fragmented, decision-making risks become inconsistent, less transparency, and more

vulnerable to short-term political pressures. For young people, this is deeply concerning. Environmental challenges operate on decades-long timelines, yet this bill weakens the institutional capacity needed to plan beyond just three-year electoral cycles. The environment should not be used as a political football to streamline government.

We are already seeing the impacts of climate change in particular regions that Southern Young Labour represent, especially across Otago and Southland. Coastal communities, infrastructure, and primary industries are increasingly exposed. Effective responses require coordination and leadership, not institutional retreat.

Weakening environmental institutions does not support growth. It creates uncertainty and risks damaging to New Zealand's clean, green international reputation. Finally, this bill sends a message to young people that environmental protection is negotiable, secondary, or even pushed aside. For many of us, climate change is a defining issue of our time.

Weakening environmental leadership risks deepening political disengagement and eroding trust in public institutions. Therefore, on behalf of Southern Young Labour, we recommend that the committee rejects this bill on the grounds that the disestablishment of the Ministry of Environment would create environmental violations that could be brushed under the carpet or lost within the new ministries of cities, environments, regions and transport.

We also believe the Secretary of Environment would not be sufficient or carry any political or legislative power to protect something that relies on a voice for protection. While we commend this attempt to introduce checks and balances, we believe that this does not go far enough. At a time of escalating environmental risk, New Zealand needs stronger leadership, not less. We need robust institutions, long-term thinking, and commitment to our future generations. Our message is simple: do not dismantle the structures that protect our future and our

mokopuna's future. Ngā mihi. Thank you. Thank you very much for that submission and for the lovely graphics as well on your submission there. Look, we're going to take one question from an MP who may be known to you, Rachel Brooking. Your electorate. From the south. Nice to see you, Liam. And the graphics for anybody watching, there's a beautiful picture of their clock tower from the University of Otago, and of course, the best electorate.

But Liam, you've talked a lot in the submission about the difference between short-term thinking and long-term thinking. So why is it that long-term thinking is important to Southern Young Labour? Long-term thinking is, many of us joined Southern Young Labour for a very transient period in our lives.

Whether it's three years at university or they could be here for long-term in Southern Young Labour. But potentially we could be inheriting a future that goes just beyond our lifetimes. It goes back to the saying of, "We plant the tree that we'll never feel the shade of." It's all about protecting something that doesn't necessarily have a voice for itself, and that's why Southern Young Labour believes in standing up for the environment, for the long-term shade, the tree that we won't feel the shade of.

Beautifully said. Thank you. Thank you very much, Liam, for your submission. Appreciate your time this afternoon. Thank you. Thank you very much. Ka kite. Bye-bye. So we now move to our next submission, Nelson Tasman Climate Forum. Who will also be joining us online this afternoon.

Fred, welcome to the select committee this afternoon. You have five minutes with us, so we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission. Thank you. Thank you, Madam Chair. Tena koutou katoa. Ko Fred Overmeyer toku ingoa. With me today is David Bartle. He has a bug, so I'll be the main speaker.

We speak on behalf of the Nelson Tasman Climate Forum, and we thank you for this important opportunity to present to you today. In our presentation, while we acknowledge, value, and improve public service integration, we'll make the case that MFE as the government's primary advisor on climate and environment needs strengthening, and that its proposed submergence through this bill in the new MCERT will have the opposite effect and will generate higher costs.

We're now on the cusp of 1.5 degree C of global temperature rise. Scientific evidence shows that global heating is accelerating. Some predict 2 degree C by 2040, unless urgent action is taken by humanity to change course. We're now rapidly approaching multiple Earth tipping points that will transform our world with devastating and irreversible consequences for people and nature.

For example, collapse of the Greenland and West Antarctic Ice Sheets, global permafrost thaw, and slowdown of the Atlantic Meridional Overturning Circulation, otherwise in short known as AMOC. Because Earth's climate system is interconnected, Aotearoa, New Zealand will not be immune to these consequences.

For example, sea level rise and the warming of our oceans as AMOC declines. The increasing frequency of severe weather events, most notably the Auckland Anniversary Weekend floods and cycling three or three years ago. But as we've also seen, a summer of storms, with tragic loss of lives, livelihoods, and massive economic costs will, in hindsight, look relatively benign events.

Noting its significance to yourselves as decision-makers, we stress the point in our written submission that most current economic assessments exclude many of the real-world impacts of climate change, such as these tipping points, sea level rise, mass migration, human health impacts, and geopolitical risks.

It's now unequivocal that the costs of climate inaction far exceeds the costs of taking climate action. We note a recent UK Climate Change Committee report finding in the last couple of weeks, that reaching net zero by 2050 would be cheaper for the UK than the single fossil fuel spike of 2022 associated with the Ukraine War.

In short, we see a strong case for strengthening MFE as the government's primary advisor on climate and environment to help us navigate the turbulent times ahead. The key foundation in all of that will be achieving net negative emissions. We fear under the bill that climate and environment will be submerged in the new MCERT, while the CEO will have the title of Secretary for the Environment. In practice, based on the budget allocations of MCERT's constituents and excluding industrial ETS allocations that in reality are subsidies for climate pollution.

We estimate climate and environment to be a little more than 6% of the CEO's responsibilities. To suggest that there will still be a Secretary for the Environment is confusing at best. Critically, the merger will submerge decisions of value judgments, trade-offs between safe long-term environments and climate, and largely short-term development considerations.

Maintaining transparency, openness, and a sense of public ownership in environmental policy is worth a great deal. Without a dedicated point of contact, public participation trust will likely plummet. We see potential for disruption to current priorities, such as the new planning legislation, climate adaptation legislation, and revision of the National Adaptation Plan.

Trade partners such as the EU, who cherish their environmental record, will be dismayed by the change. To conclude, the Nelson Tasman Climate Forum strongly opposes the disestablishment of the Ministry for the Environment for reasons of transparency, accountability, and its role for restoring our climate, protecting nature to tire, and protecting future generations.

However, if the committee is of a mind to recommend the bill proceed, and in a departure from our written submission, we recommend broadly following the Environmental Law Initiative proposalTo establish the Secretary for the Environment as Deputy Chief Executive of the new ministry with an exclusive mandate and sufficient resources for its climate and environment responsibilities, and to provide direction that the new ministry must prioritize our national climate obligations and environmental outcomes across all functions.

Thank you, Madam Chair. We welcome any questions. Thank you very much, Fred. Look, you have met time with that submission, so there aren't any further questions. But look, we thank you for your time today, and it doesn't actually look like Nelson there behind you either . I'm sure it's sunny down there today Much less here in Antarctica.
Yes. Thank you.

Now we welcome our next submitter, Parliamentary Commissioner for the Environment, and I understand the team is here, and we have Simon joining us online from afar as well. So just to acknowledge you all here today. We'll just wait for Simon, the commissioner, to pop up.

He was there. He popped up and then popped away again. Keeps coming and going. You have a problem. There we go. Welcome, Commissioner, and welcome all to the select committee. You have 10 minutes with us on this, so we'll hand it over to you to talk us through your submission. So thank you.

Thanks, Madam Chair. Look, there's no right way to arrange government departments, so anything can be made to work. And if you want to get rid of the Ministry for the Environment, this is, from a legal point of view, an elegant way. So that's good. However, in my view, a separate ministry remains the best way to ensure that environmental problems are properly addressed.

Unlike domains such as transport, housing or infrastructure, where there are numerous private and public owners, operators and interest groups, the environment doesn't have anyone that can claim to own it. All of us are dependent on the environment of which we're a part. Now, in my view, it makes sense to have an agency whose job it is to ensure that the consequences for the environment of all our other policy interventions are properly understood.

If we can justify a separate ministry for regulation, we can certainly justify a separate ministry for the environment. Now, as the officer of Parliament charged with keeping under review the system of processes and agencies that make up our environmental management system, my advice to you is that this change is unlikely to help the environment, and I want to underline three points briefly.

First, bundling these agencies together undermines an important principle of public sector advice, and that is the need for contestability so that elected officials, in other words, ministers, not public servants, can confront and make the big trade-offs. There's a real risk with a mega ministry that trade-offs will be resolved within the organization, and that the differences will not be aired with ministers, where accountability for those trade-offs should rightly sit.

Now, neither the previous nor the current government have made a convincing case, in my view, that to reform the RMA you need two acts. But if you are all agreed that that's the right approach, then surely it would make sense to have different ministries responsible for the two different acts to ensure that the trade-offs between them are properly presented.

Frankly, I don't have a lot of confidence in large, internal, bureaucratic trade-off exercises. A Ministry for the Environment overseeing a Natural Environment Act would better ensure free and frank advice than a mega ministry trying to reach a compromise it hopes all of its ministers can swallow.

There's also a transparency point that you as a select committee should seek advice on. Right now, the Ministry for the Environment has two appropriations, one for environment, one for climate. You need to find out in this merged ministry if there is going to be an appropriation for those things. With my EEE, which I bring to you once a year, my estimate of environmental expenditure, I can tell you what's being spent on those items from the budget.

I may not be able to do so anywhere near so easily if this is all just put into a big blender and you can't see what's being spent on the environment. Okay, my second point. If you believe that a smaller number of mega ministries is better, then you should at least be prepared to consider merging MFE with MPI.

Now, that may shock some people, and it's not my first preference. After all, many of our most pressing environmental problems are associated with primary industries like water quality, greenhouse gas emissions and erosion. So I suppose there could be a problem for free and frank advice about those problems if you put those together.

But look at it another way. The current proposal is to create a very urban-focused ministry. It seems to have been the inspiration of people who live in cities and see the environment as being all about cars and houses and urban design and infrastructure. But the vast bulk of the natural environment is outside of cities.

Most of New Zealand is rural, and I think rural people are much closer to the natural environment. They work in it. Forests, farms, vineyards, they all rely directly on the health of the environment and climate. And MPI understands that. It's got serious technical capabilities in areas like biosecurity and animal health that are much closer to the natural environment than people advising on how many apartments you can squeeze around a bus stop.Now, I worry that this agency won't understand rural New Zealand and the people who live and work there.

In short, the merger facilitated by this bill treats the environment as though it were a branch of planning law. It isn't. The environment intersects with every corner of public policy. Which brings me to my third and final point. If ever there was a time to avoid an upheaval, it's in the middle of a massive legislative reform, which you as a committee are overseeing. Officials will already have their hands full trying to make complex new legislation work and develop national direction across a bewildering array of topics. It's the worst time for

people to be sizing up new managers and reapplying for their jobs. Furthermore, the government has decided to upend regional councils, which will cause even more uncertainty for the implementation of new law. And as I've said in respect to that reform, form should follow function. The same applies here.

If there's a need to tinker with the departmental Lego set, it should happen after new functions have been determined and legislated for. So there you are. That is my frank advice to you on this initiative. Well, thank you, Simon, for that very passionate submission.

Look, you've opened up a flurry of questions around the table here. So look, we will start with Rachel. Well, we know that we can always rely on frank advice from you. Thank you. But I've just got one question to start with, and that is, you're talking about this contestability and ministers going head-to-head on issues, and the word trade-off is used a lot, and I know it's one I use a lot as well.

But can you give us some examples of the type of trade-offs that you are imagining there? There's an unlimited number, and I'm sure you can imagine them. But you take transport, for instance. You've got climate consequences, you've got congestion consequences, you've got economic consequences, you've got social consequences. What's public, what's private?

Or you take water. You need water for irrigation. You need water for drinking. You need water to protect the ecology. These are all complicated issues where different parts of our society, whether we're urban dwellers or rural, whether we're after water for industry or we're trying to protect a local park or whatever, we've all got claims on this thing called the environment. And the environment hasn't got anyone who can say, "Well, actually, I own the environment, and I can give you my view." You need a ministry, or if you're going to have this mega one, you need a

very clearly defined entity that can provide specific advice on the environment. And if I just underline this point again, if you can't even tell how much money you're spending on that in a blurred ministry, I think you should be concerned. Thank you. And Francisco.

Thank you, Chair. Would you agree with the advice that the government has gotten around this, that given the proposed agency would be development oriented, there's a particular risk of environment functions and perspectives being lost? And if you agree with that, how would you recommend they mitigate that? Inevitably, there will be that tension brought in-house.

That tension exists, I remind you, right now around the Cabinet table where ministers are discussing things. What, as I've just said, what you're doing by pulling this all together is that you make the tensions and the trade-offs less transparent. There isn't an agency which can give clear advice just from an environmental point of view. We all accept there have to be trade-offs, and we all accept that development will involve trade-offs.

We need to know what they are. It needs to come from an agency that can provide that clearly and transparently. I think it's easiest done if you have a separate ministry for the environment. Thank you. Thank you, Simon. Look, we'll go to Grant now. Yeah. G'day, Simon. You highlight, for example, will the new ministry, the super ministry, understand rural New Zealand? Okay.

If that's where we end up with the new ministry, which is where we're heading, what advice could you give to help prevent that happening? Any thoughts in that space? I don't have advice for that because I think you'd make it very difficult for yourselves. You will have an entity which is heavily focused on the spatial planning side of things and the urban side.

Now look, that is important. You've heard me be critical of two acts, but one good thing about spatial planning is that we can bring stuff together. The problem is that you have put that in an agency alongside the entity which is looking after the environment and a lot of that rural stuff. You've put that all together.

So it's hard to see why the weight of stuff won't go in that direction. This is just a straight contestability issue. And I wasn't being facetious when I said why not look at MPI, MFE. I mean, I could understand the case for wanting fewer ministries and fewer chief executive salaries, I suppose, a few other things. But rural New Zealand is living face to face with all of those challenges around water and biodiversity and so on and so forth.

And actually, I'm very impressed by MPI in many ways. I think it's an agency with serious technical skill. Whatever technical skill exists inside this ministry won't be technical skill around the natural environment in the way that an agency like MPI can bring to the table. But again, if you're going to go down this road, I think it's very important that you can see transparently what the appropriation is for environment and also for climate, by the way, so you know what's being spent and you can drill this major domo who's going to be running it all, the chief executive, you can drill

that person on, okayHow are you mobilising the resources we're giving you for the environment? If that's all lost in a single appropriation, I think your job gets even harder as a select committee. I might just jump in there, Simon, because you talked about departmental Lego set. I think it's the wrong way to think about it, how could we arrange the Lego? I'd look at form and function. What are you trying to achieve?

Okay, thank you, Simon. One last question from Priyanka. I don't agree with that. DOC is an operational department. It's a big, big, big land manager, and it needs a lot of real technical advice and land management advice.

Now they're looking after tracks, they're looking after tourism concessions, all of that stuff. It is a real operational department. What we're talking about here are policy departments. Mm. And I think it's a question of deciding what policy areas you should pull together, and there may well be other agencies you could put with MFE if you wanted to.
